speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel seven of the interview with Henry Rosmarin. Henry, it's late 1944. And you were talking about being at Camp Funfteichen. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- end of December-- second half of December, 1944, again, rumors are about the war, landings in Europe, and allies fighting in Europe. But we really had no idea where they are, what country they liberated. And all we could do is-- is listen to what the Germans are telling us. And we knew, of course, that wasn't true. But then the rumors were that-- that they were going to evacuate us again. They called it evacuation, but it was actually a death march. So they marched us to another camp. They-- one morning, you-- you-- you don't need to pack anything at this point because you-- you have no possessions. You have a spoon and a-- and a bowl, you know, which you carry with you in case you-- you come into some food-- they distribute food. So-- so when they say assemble, you assemble. When they whistle, you-- you get up. When they-- when they whistle again and tell you, disperse, you disperse. You became a non-- just a-- a functioning person taking-- following-- following orders. And you even-- even to-- to relieve yourself, you have to ask permission to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're standing on this assembly platzen-- assembly place. I'm saying 'platzen.' I'm thinking German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And orders-- they start shouting orders, forwards march. Opening the gates. We're marching together. [? Oh ?] [? gosh. ?] Snow on the ground. We're marching. And we marched for probably, let's see, that day, all day. They gave us some rations, some bread, which we-- some of us ate. I remember I didn't eat mine. Again, I was mindful of my-- of my sugar and it-- my sugar dropping. So I let myself a few-- a few bites of bread and just kind of rationed myself so I'll have on the-- on the road to eat. And-- and for water, we would substitute snow. And we marched until sundown. And it's-- and we're-- there was a big barn. They herded us like a bunch of cattle or sheep into a barn. But the barn could only hold so many. And by the time my turn came, the doors were shut.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we must have been probably 200 or 300 left. So we spent the night outside, which in a way, it was probably better because I heard there were a lot of killings inside and a lot-- a lot of hoarding and stealing. You know, they would cut someone's throat for a piece of bread or-- so we-- I remember waking-- waking up the next day-- the next day at dawn and-- and covered with snow, the first time in my life, I was-- I was ever covered in-- with snow instead of a blanket. And we got up and marched farther and not think about no colds, no sniffles, no, you know, no aspirins. And we marched until we came-- this march took about almost three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to a camp called the [GERMAN], which was the-- we were all satellite camps under that particular camp, which was called Gross Rosen. And that was a-- a death camp with crematoria. And-- and all these camps that I described previously I-- were under the authority of that. This was like a mother camp, you know, a [GERMAN], the main camp. There, this was a horrible place, just like a warehouse. There was no-- no more going to work, no regular-- that camp made-- made the regular camp look-- look-- look like Club Med, to-- to-- to use a comparison, because there-- it-- we had, there were like three-- three bunks high in the one camp. And this-- and this particular one, we had five bunks high. And-- and it was like shelves-- no mattresses anymore, no straw mattresses, just-- just shelves. You know, you have five-- five people in the-- in the one opening, and five in the other, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from that camp, after about two weeks or so, we were, again, asked to-- to assemble, marched to the railroad station, put again in boxcars. This time, it was an open boxcar, like a coal car without-- just a open boxcar. And it kept snowing. But this-- this was early, like February, middle of February, I-- I would guess. Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of year of-- yeah, thank you for reminding me. For viewer, this was 1945 already. And they took us to Buchenwald. After about three, three and a half days' journey, I wind up in Buchenwald. I had some episodes on the-- on the road. I fainted a couple times. It must've been my sugar. But a couple of my buddies, we used to hang around together. We had like a buddy system. You'd stick around with-- with somebody. You know, they would help you, and you would help him. In case of need, it was easier to-- to-- to-- to pull through. And he gave me some snow or something to lick for-- and I came to again. But there was no water and no food for perhaps three or more days. And we finally came to Buchenwald, they put us in a big tent, I remember. And by that time, we had no washing pail, no change of laundry, nothing. And I-- I was itching good. And there were plenty-- I had other-- plenty of little-bitty cohabitants, little ones that would cohabitate with me. And they would put us in a tent, like a circus tent. They took us to [GERMAN], which means delousing, actually, what word literally means. And from there, took us to showers, actual showers. And then I remember, in-- in the shower, I almost lost my life, too, because I fainted again when the hot water and the steam hit me. I just-- I just fainted. And before I knew-- I don't know how much time expired, but I was told that I was on a stack of bodies already stacked up at the entrance of the showers. They-- they just threw me on the stack. But I was still alive, until this buddy-- my-- I told you, we had a buddy system-- told the capo, he says, he's alive. He just fainted. He faints a lot. So-- so they picked me up. And it was a-- God bless him, if the-- if the person is still alive, and if he isn't, the One Above should be kind to-- to-- to the person. It was a Czech. I later found out that the Czech underground was very, very strong in Buchenwald. And he spoke Czech to me, and I answered in Polish. And we're-- and I told him, [POLISH]. He brought me some of the coffee, [? something. ?] And he poured it into my throat. And he gave me a piece of bread. And I-- I-- I devoured it quickly and somehow came to myself. And he saved my life again because of-- of the showers and everything, you know, just, you know, just the ground gave from under me. And Buchenwald was as-- a lot of death camp. Again, in-- in regular times-- when I say regular times, in-- in the other camps, we would march to work. And you could have-- have some-- we called it organize. Organizing means you-- you-- you-- you-- in a way, it means stealing a little bit, too, because if-- if someone-- if the SS [? not was ?] looking and you were loading potatoes, you put some on pocket or start eating. So it was stealing. But it's called-- we-- every-- every former concentration camp inmate you will speak to will say organizing-- to organize some food. So you could-- you know, there were some-- all kind of chances in the regular camps. But during the death marches, and in those transit camps, it was a chaos already. Buchenwald in those days held-- my number in Buchenwald was 200 and some thousand. I don't even remember-- I remember my regular number. But I don't remember exactly my regular-- my Buchenwald number. I think it's 245,000 something. But anyway, and I knew I had to get out of this Buchenwald somehow. I-- I-- I saw horrible conditions. Rumors of cannibalism. I never saw actual ones. But I saw one time a bunch of Ukrainians or perhaps Belarusians. They were behind a barrack. They were cooking something. It wasn't-- it wasn't a beef, that's for sure. And it smelled awful, and two or three guys were kind of hanging around. And one of them would say, he say, don't tell me he's going-- he's-- I think he's going to eat that. Let me have a bite. Let me have a bite. And I just wondered what-- I don't know. It was-- it's either a rodent or-- or who knows what. [? And it was ?] strictly starvation. One time-- the meals were not anymore three times. You wind up-- line up in-- in front of, of-- of all place, a movie theater. In those days, when the Germans first-- first built Buchenwald, they also built a movie theater for because that was sort of a show camp and for most political prisoners, German political prisoners, communists. So they built that-- built a movie-- a regular movie theater. They were showing all kind of propaganda movies. But by the time that Buchenwald got to be so overpopulated, they converted it into living and-- and cooking quarters. So we would line up in front of the so-called theater. And once a day, we sat maybe in line two or three hours a day before you got your daily soup and two or three potatoes. And-- and you just constantly hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- how can I get out of Buchenwald? Then I heard someone say they looking for volunteers. They looking for-- for what they call [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] in German means trades-- tradespeople and that that particular call was for-- for electricians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was not an electrician. Never had the opportunity to learn. But I worked with-- with-- you know, on construction with some electricians. And I remember, I used to hand them different-- different tools. And one of them was a-- a-- a-- a-- [GERMAN], I remember. It was a-- what, like a big scissors, a tool that would bend pipes. And little did I know that this one tool may save my life again. Everything is small miracles, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as they called for a tradesman, I-- I-- I had enough courage to-- to line up. I'm also a tradesman. I'm an electrician.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea what-- I-- all I-- all I did is helping hand a tool to a real electrician on the construction. And he was up there on a ladder. And I would help him, you know, and hand-- hand his tool or-- or carry something for him, like a helper. But I figured maybe I can bluff my way through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'll never forget this. This is 1944 now, end of-- end of February, perhaps, beginning of March. And there was two SS men sitting in front of a table and at the big assembly place. And inmates are lined up, maybe 50, maybe 75 inmates. And they have different-- different tools laid out on the table. I'm telling you this story just to prove what-- that it takes all kinds of little miracles to-- to-- to-- to fall, so to say, to fall through the cracks, you know? And my turn came. And he says, [GERMAN]? I said, [GERMAN]. I am one. And he says, [? can ?] identify the tools? I says, yes. So he says, what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he picks up-- and I couldn't believe my eyes. He picked up a tool, which I knew by the name. And it was-- it was like gardener's scissors. And I says, [GERMAN], you know, standing at attention. He said, [GERMAN]. I guessed it right. That qualified me as an electrician. And I was sent to another camp called Schoenbeck, S-C-H-O-E-N-B-E-C-K. It's 75 kilometers, I remember, exactly because I used to march through that road. It said Magdeburg, which I knew that town by its name from-- from historic references. Magdeburg, 75 kilometers. So it was that close, and-- and-- and Berlin was, I think, 120 or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they shipped us. And the next day, I saw my name on a list, and I reported to the-- to the guards. And I was shipped to this time by train also, also boxcars. But it wasn't a long ride. It was in one day. And we came to this tech camp of Schoenbeck again. Same routine. Admission, you know-- given a-- a number, uniform, and a-- a job assignment. And I said to myself, now the truth will come out. And what am I going to do? They put me-- they put me on-- and it turn-- as it turn out to be, these were air crafts-- electric Henkels. I didn't find out until after liberation, they were making parts for-- you remember the Messerschmidt Junkers and then Henkels? These were the air crafts that made Junkers. J-U-- it's-- it's pronounced in German, 'Yun-kers.' And in English, it's 'Jun-kers,' J-U-N-K-E-R-S.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- and I said to myself, this time, I'm really-- this is going to be the end for me because they'll find out that I-- I was some kind of imposter. But the war for-- this is typical German-- German mentality. March of 1944, the-- the Russians are 200 kilometers from Berlin. The Americans are nearing the Elbe. You know, and it's-- the-- the war can be counted in days now. We had no idea, but they did. And they still go through the motions of-- of qualifying you, of, you know, shipping you and of-- of making exams whether you're a real electrician. And-- and you know, and segregating and putting in separates-- and [? well, ?] they took us to these big hangers like. And one guy, he was telling us what we're going to do here, you know. And then we went back to the camp. Next day, we never showed up. We never saw the hangars again. They were bombed out, burned out. The-- the allies were bombing like mad in-- in the last few days of war. So I never got to-- to prove my-- my prowess of-- of-- of being a electrician, which was also a-- a good thing because I would have probably gotten severely disciplined, and probably [? got ?] beat up and killed, maybe. But we did dig ditches again, trenches, anti-tanks, and, you know, went to town of Schoenbeck to clear rubbish and bombed-out houses. And again, organizing came in handy. I'd jump in the cellar, look for some-- sometimes we would find the jars of apples or tomatoes or sauerkraut, you know, in jars. And we would-- we would eat some and then suffer through it because you would-- you would have stomach problem, you know. But-- but it-- it was a way of getting-- or carrots, anything we could find, just anything to eat. This took another-- this is March now of 1944. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, March of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. March of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Schoenbeck.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the-- the-- the camp that they sent-- that-- that I went from Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm trying to, you know, to kind of bring things to a conclusion because it's-- it's hard to-- to put everything, you know, on one tape. But I know I'm-- I'm skipping perhaps a-- a few things. But I'm trying to get to the important events, even though I remember small ones too. And now, we in, as I said, March of 1944. And obviously, the-- the front is nearing to-- to this town of Schoenbeck. Schoenbeck, I didn't know until we start marching again, that it was right on-- at the-- on the Elbe River. In fact, at the river, the town was one-- half of it one side, now half on the other side. The Elbe-- E-L-B-E-- which was then the demarcation line between the Russian troops and the American troops.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day-- and I'll never forget that date because I later on asked the guard, what day is this today? Again, towards the evening, they blow the whistle. [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: You come to the assemble place again in the camp, and they say marching orders again. We did get a-- a ration of bread, next day's bread ration, which I pocketed quickly behind my-- I hid behind my shirt. And I ate half on the-- on the road because I figured I might march all through the night. And I'll save something for later.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're marching-- and-- and as we're coming close to the Elbe River, I see German troops, Wehrmacht, guarding the entrance of it and rushing us, [? too. ?] And the SS guards kept telling us, schnell, schnell, schnell. This time we didn't-- we-- we-- all of a sudden, we're-- we're picking up the pace. We're not anymore marching. We're jogging and running, already, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And with wooden shoes, it's hard to run. And-- and, you know, so you slip and you, you know, fall. And you can't run. And he kept saying, schnell, schnell. I didn't realize until after we went through, and I kind of looked back. And I saw a-- a bunch of green uniformed Germans crawling under the bridge and throwing wires and unwinding and rolling wires. There was something going on there. And as they-- as they rushed us along after we passed cross the Elbe River, we were not perhaps maybe a half a mile away, I heard a horrible explosion. And the ground shook on us. And I looked back, and there was smoke billowed from-- from the river. They blew up the bridge. And I said, oh, wait a minute. I'd start thinking, if they were blowing up the bridge, and this guy, the SS man, who was-- who was marching along us-- I-- I-- I must also tell you, in those days, we had the SS, the guards along were-- were also older people. They were not anymore the young SS guys. They were either Lithuanian nationals, Ukrainian, German sympathizers, or German national [GERMAN], or they were old people from the old guard. And they couldn't march very well either, themselves. So-- so they would stop, you know, for some water and stop to take a rest because they were tired themselves. They-- and one time, we stopped for some water. And this guy put his gun down. And he, all of a sudden, I keep watching him. And he took off a pair of pants, took his boots off, and took off his military pants, and put civilian pants underneath, and covered it with a green coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told my two friends, I said, hey, what's going on here? This guy's changing into civilian uniform. And they blew up the bridge. I mean, how many-- how many clues do you need? This-- we-- we could be overrun by the Allied troops. You know, I mean, maybe we should make a run for it. And they say, no. Listen, after-- so after five and a half years, you're going to get killed now running? Tomorrow we could be liberated. They're going to catch up with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, you know, maybe you're right. So I marched along another two hours, another three. But-- but towards must have been midnight, maybe 1 o'clock, I couldn't march anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told my two friends, I says, I don't know about you guys, but I-- the next stop we make for-- for water, for a rest stop in the next village, I'm going to make a run for it. Are we on the same reel? Anyway, you were saying, Henry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as I was saying, the guy is changing his-- his trousers into civilian. And-- and by the way, when I said '44, I meant '45. You see, I'm 73, and, well, you can't-- you can't cheat Father Time. How could I be thinking of '44? It is '45. He's changing into civilian trousers. The Allies are close to the Elbe River. They blow up the bridge behind us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is-- you know, how many-- how many more clues do we need? The Allied troops are behind us. We-- we could be liberated tonight yet. But the next two stops, my strength and my-- my feet were swollen already and were beginning-- I was beginning to lose power, so to-- so to speak. So I told my two friends, I say, you know, I don't believe I'm-- I can't go on [? to ?] like this. They say, Henry, hold on. This-- this could be it. By-- by day-- by daybreak, we could be overrun by the troops. They're in-- they in tanks and motorized, and we marching. How long will it take them to-- to catch up with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is true. What we didn't know, that the Elbe-- they-- they had orders to stop west of the-- of the Elbe River. We were already on the east bank of the Elbe River. And the-- somehow, it was made up in Potsdam whenever any boundaries were drawn between Stalin, Roosevelt, Churchill, and the French President that the Elbe River is going to be demarcation line, which plays a big-- a big-- which made a big difference in my-- in my liberation because the American and English troops stopped at the Elbe River. They didn't cross it. They waited for the Russians to come in from the east. And they made an agreement to-- to unite in that particular village, [GERMAN]. And it's a known fact now about how it all happened. But I had no-- no idea. So I figured they-- my friends were telling me, discouraging me from-- from trying make a break because I-- I might get killed in-- at-- at the very last moment. But I had no choice. It was not [? really ?] an act of courage. It was a act of desperation. And as I said, I had no more food. Water was the only sustenance I could get on the next stop. So I figured, the next village we come, I'll try to make a break for it. We came to a little village. I don't know-- don't know the name. It-- it was too dark to read, and I wasn't even interested.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I do remember talking to the guard who changed-- you know, I told-- we were all German. And I asked him, what day is today? And he says, well, why you-- what do you want to know? You got an appointment or some kind or something? And you know, he laughing. And I says, no, I just want to know because my birthday. It's April, isn't it? And this was April now in 1945, of course. And he-- I says, it's April, isn't it? He says, yeah, it's April. The 9th, if you-- if you must know. And I says, yeah, yeah, oh, the 9th. He said, when is your birthday? Actually, my birthday wasn't until October. I says, well, I still got a few days' time. So we kept marching and marching. And I said to myself, 9th of April. If-- if I live through tonight, I will celebrate my actual birthday that day. And I, you know, silently started praying to God. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was thinking, what am I going to do? [? I am ?] striped uniform. I have no papers, wooden shoes. I can hardly walk. Who-- if I do make a run for it, how am I going to hide? Oh, what-- what's the difference? Tomorrow, the-- the Allieds-- either French or English or somebody will be here to liberate us. So just make a break for it. I know I can't-- I can't walk and I-- so I decided, I says to the other guys, I said, this village we're coming, we-- in the next one, I-- I'm-- I'm try to make a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say, Henry, don't do this [? all the way. ?] Anyway, make a long story short, I-- they didn't want to come with me. And I said, fine, wish me luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we did a-- we came to the village square. Some locals came out with buckets of water and ladles. And they would let, like, five guys go and five come back. And if you wanted to relieve yourself-- always in fives, you know-- they would count. And sometimes they would-- they would be too tired to count already. So I just looked around, and my turn-- turn came to go get some water. And I had to step perhaps like from here to the door, maybe from the square to the-- to the nearest sidewalk maybe 30, 40 feet. So I'm walking over there with the-- the other guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as my turn came, she put the water. And I-- I looked over my shoulder quick. And I didn't see any. I saw the guard had his back turned and talking to some other guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got rid of the water quick. And the lady stood in the doorway with a bucket of water and a ladle, with her legs spread like this, with the bucket between her legs. And I-- as I looked back, I wanted to get inside the door. But she stood in the doorway. So I crawled. I put my head between her feet and spread and pushed away a little bit. And I says, please, bitte, bitte, bitte. And I-- and I start, you know, crawling into the doorway. And she squeeze quick and got my head caught in-- in between her feet and-- and-- and said, and I remember, in Russian, [RUSSIAN]. You stupid! What-- what in the world are you doing? You get us both killed, in pure Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I could see, oh, this is not a German woman. So I start talking Polish to her and some broken Russian, you know. Please let me in. And-- but-- but I didn't wait for her answer. I was just [? spreading. ?] And already, before I knew it, I kept crawling. I was already inside the hallway-- dark, pitch dark and quiet. I'm in the hallway then. And I can't get believe I am away from the column. But I got to-- but I can't stay here [? into the ?] hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got to-- and I know it was-- I knew it was a two-story building. So I-- in the dark, I'm-- I'm-- I'm trying to touch. And I found steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I slowly go up the step. And I found a landing between steps-- the landing, turn around, and again step, up steps to the-- to the-- what Europe-- in Europe was called the first floor. It was actually the second in American terms. And I came to this first floor up there. And there was a window going out to the street. And I looked down. And I almost fainted. I see the-- the guys, my friends, and they're standing in a column, you know, sipping water and waiting for orders to march. And I'm away from them. Now I got to hide some place. And then-- and I'm-- so I knock on the door. There were two doors, one to the left, one to the right. I knock on the one to the left. No one answered.

speaker: interviewee
text: One to the right, and somebody opened the door, and said, ja? And I says, bitte. [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy looked at me, and he says, Jesus Maria. [POLISH]. He started speaking Polish to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to myself, oh my god. This is a miracle. The guy is a-- is a [? [POLISH], ?] a Polish national. He was a forced laborer. And he-- and he says, please, get away. Please, get away. I says, well, give me-- I said, can you please hide me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no, I have children here. Please, go-- go-- go away. I-- I'll-- I'll give you some food. Just go away. Go, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but where? So-- so he threw-- threw a pair of pants at me. He says, please go. And I said, how about some food? So he threw a piece of bread at me. And he says, go, go upstairs. So I thought he meant upstairs, maybe somebody else lives there, and he-- he's going to help me. So I come upstairs also, next floor. I'm two stories high now. I'm looking down again. And-- and I can hear him talking. By this time, I hear also say, [GERMAN], which means, enter in formation, you know, because in that-- in that particular order, when we went after water, we were in formation of under the orders of, like, at ease, you know? But now it's [GERMAN], which means get in formation. You're getting ready to march again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I'm knocking again on the door on the left, right. Nobody opens. And I see again steps leading to the-- through-- to-- to the ceiling. And I see a trap door on the ceiling. And I'm beginning to touch in the dark again and see if I could, [? you ?] [? know, ?] find a pair-- a set of steps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, let's stop for a moment while we change reels.
